sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 35, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 34 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The batch stopped after 2 actions (LEFT, LEFT) because the second LEFT was no_impact. Let me analyze the current state:
- Player white extension at col 20 (node 2: W, pixels=4, boundary=[[28, 20], [31, 20]])
- Player green body at cols 21-23 (node 3: N, pixels=12, boundary=[[28, 21], [28, 23], [31, 23], [31, 21]])
- Left blue square frame at rows 28-31, cols 24-27 (node 4: c, pixels=12)
- Left blue center at rows 29-30, cols 17-18 (node 6)
- Carried blue center at rows 29-30, cols 25-26 (node 7)
- Bar at rows 28-31, cols 28-39
Wait, something changed! The white extension is now on the LEFT side of the green body (col 20), not the right! And the left blue square frame moved to cols 24-27 (it was at cols 16-19 before). 
Also, the carried blue center is at cols 25-26, which is INSIDE the left blue square frame (cols 24-27). And the left blue center is still at cols 17-18.
So it seems like:
1. The player's white extension flipped to the LEFT side (col 20)
2. The left blue square frame moved to cols 24-27
3. The carried blue center is now inside the left blue square frame
This is very interesting. It seems like the player's "facing" direction changed. Previously the white extension was on the right, now it's on the left. This might have happened because the player moved LEFT past the left blue square frame, causing the frame to "wrap around" or the player to "flip" direction.
Actually, looking more carefully, I think what happened is:
- The player moved LEFT from cols 20-23 to cols 16-19 (first LEFT)
- The frame was at cols 16-19, so the player overlapped with it
- The second LEFT was blocked (no_impact) because the player couldn't move further left
- The white extension flipped to the left side (col 20)
Wait, but the player is at cols 20-23 (white at 20, green at 21-23). The frame is at cols 24-27. So the player is to the LEFT of the frame, with the white extension on the left side. This means the player is now "facing" left.
Hmm, but if the player is facing left, then SPACE would extend the white part to the left, not to the right. The frame is to the RIGHT of the player, so SPACE wouldn't collect it.
Wait, let me reconsider. Maybe the white extension always extends in the direction opposite to the green body. When the white extension is on the left (col 20) and the green body is on the right (cols 21-23), SPACE would extend the white part further left. But the frame is to the right, so SPACE wouldn't help.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the mechanic. Let me look at what happened at the right blue square:
- Player was at cols 40-43 (green at 40-42, white at 43)
- Frame was at cols 44-47
- White extension was on the RIGHT, frame was on the RIGHT
- SPACE extended white to the right, collecting the blue center
At the left blue square now:
- Player is at cols 20-23 (white at 20, green at 21-23)
- Frame is at cols 24-27
- White extension is on the LEFT, frame is on the RIGHT
- SPACE would extend white to the left, away from the frame
So the player is facing the wrong way. I need to get the white extension on the RIGHT side of the green body, facing the frame.
But how do I flip the player's direction? Maybe I need to move RIGHT past the frame, which would flip the white extension to the right side.
Let me try moving RIGHT to get past the frame, then moving back LEFT to position the player correctly.
Actually wait - let me re-examine. The player is at cols 20-23 (white at 20, green at 21-23). The frame is at cols 24-27. If I move RIGHT, the player goes to cols 24-27, overlapping with the frame. If I move RIGHT again, the player goes to cols 28-31, past the frame. Then the white extension might flip to the right side.
But actually, I'm not sure about the flipping mechanic. Let me just try pressing SPACE to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 28-31, cols 20-23 (white at 20, green at 21-23)
# Left blue square frame at rows 28-31, cols 24-27
# White extension is on the LEFT, frame is on the RIGHT
# Let me try pressing SPACE to see what happens

result = action(['SPACE'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After SPACE:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
print("Score:", result.get('score'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
